comments.text: After a longer back and forth with GPT-5.4 I've got some new partial results in the direction I wanted, namely: L-packets as an explanation for "hidden signal". Here's the note that proves the following: If the classical Ulam sequence \(U(1,2)\) really has positive upper density, then the strange Fourier spike people see numerically is not a fluke. After passing to a Furstenberg limit, that spike turns into an actual eigenfrequency of a shift system built from the sequence. So the “hidden signal” in the Ulam numbers gets upgraded from an experiment to a real structural feature of a limiting dynamical system. The key finite combinatorial input is that the initial segments \(A_{N} = U(1,2)\cap [1,N]\) contain very few ordered Schur triples \(x+y=z\), in fact at most \(3|A_{N}|\). By Green’s theorem, that means \(A_{N}\) is very close to a genuinely sum-free set \(B_{N}\). Informally, the Ulam sequence is behaving a lot like an almost sum-free set, and that is what forces a big Fourier coefficient to appear. The last part explains what this says about the preferred Fourier phases. Those maximizing phases cannot wander all over \(\mathbb{T} = \mathbb{R}/2\pi\mathbb{Z}\); any limit point has to come from a finite spectral atom set. In the irrational case, the paper even gets a circle-factor picture and a non-uniform limiting phase distribution for the Ulam numbers and for the nearby sum-free cores. So the overall message is: assuming positive density, the Ulam sequence has real hidden harmonic structure, not just mysterious numerics.

Good read and nice results as well. I was curious, how long does it typically take you to go from starting the chat / your attempt on the problem and ending off on a result? Have you found patterns such as NT problems taking shorter for results, comments with less problems being more approachable, etc? Thanks in advance.

Thanks, there are no rules I could find for open problems - very rarely you get an LLM finding the strategy right away to solve a problem. I had some problems with interesting results being produced in a couple of hours (extended thinking usually takes at least 30-40 minutes per prompt). But for example this one took me days in January, then I stopped, and revisited it when GPT-5.4 came around and it took me around 5 days of prompting back and forth and multiple conversations, and then checking. Plus in this one I "forced" LLM to look at the problem the way I want - from Langlands perspective. So the strategy came from me. There's a lot of exploration, starting conversations over, when LLM gets stuck, re-sharing .tex notes.

Thank you, it's really helpful to hear the strategy came from you and the model executed within that frame. It entirely restructures what I thought was doable with models. Two things I'm most curious about: 1. When you pushed GPT-5.4 toward Langlands, did it resist or give reasons why it "shouldn't" work before engaging? I've found that models often confidently explain why an unconventional approach is wrong; right up until you force them through and it works. 2. How do you decide when to force through a seemingly unpromising direction versus pivoting? In my experience the model's first instinct is often wrong for cross-domain problems, but I haven't found a reliable signal for when my own unconventional instinct is right versus when I'm just being stubborn. The Ulam result is elegant, the almost sum-free connection forcing the Fourier coefficient is a beautiful bridge. Thanks for your responses.

Re 1: it didn't really resist, but it took some time to get to interesting results (and actually GPT-5.4, 5.2 couldn't do it). Re 2: again there's no rule, I'm pivoting mostly when I feel stuck. Usually if I'm not getting anything interesting - even small - within 5-7 prompts then I'm looking for a pivot.

Congrats. Standard check finds no issues in this one. I'm adding an experimental step where I ask GPT for interesting comments on the paper after the check, hoping maybe it'll lead to further inspiration(?). No idea if that's useful, though!
Thanks, these extra remarks can be very useful. Very good idea!

Might be interesting to try (with GPT) optimizing further the constant $1.454$ (Theorem 1 in F. Clément and S. Steinerberger 2025 paper) as the methods there are "short". That said, does anyone know what kind of an asymptotic do we "expect" for the sequence - might be a known thing, but I was not able to find anything online?

Actually there's a lot of cool numerical computations in A Hidden Signal in the Ulam sequence by Steinerberger, especially related to the title hidden signal which comes from Fourier series, and here's something worth computing. I had this idea coming from Langlands program: Let $(a_n)_{n\ge 1}$ be the Ulam sequence with $a_1=1$, $a_2=2$, and for $n\ge 2$ let $a_{n+1}$ be the least integer $>a_n$ that has a unique representation $a_i+a_j$ with $1\le i<j\le n$. For each $n$ write $A_n=\{a_1,\dots,a_n\}$ and view $A_n$ as a subset of the cyclic group $\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z$ by choosing a prime $p>2a_n$ (so that there is no wraparound for sums in $[0,2a_n]$). Define the discrete Fourier transform of the indicator $1_{A_n}$ by $S_n(k)=\sum_{x\in A_n} e^{2\pi i kx/p}$ for $k\in\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z$. Numerically there is a striking ''hidden signal'': for large $n$ one finds a frequency $k_n^*\in\{1,\dots,(p-1)/2\}$ maximizing $|S_n(k)|$ such that the associated angle $\alpha_n=2\pi k_n^*/p$ stabilizes near an irrational constant $\alpha\approx 2.571447\ldots$, and the normalized peak height $|S_n(k_n^*)|/n$ remains macroscopic (around $0.7$-$0.8$ at the scales tested). A convenient way to package this phenomenon is via an $L$-packet (harmonic packet) hypothesis: not only is the fundamental mode $k_n^*$ large, but several harmonics $mk_n^*$ are simultaneously large, i.e. $|S_n(mk_n^*)|/n$ is far above the random baseline $n^{-1/2}$ for $1\le m\le L$. In our computations, for many values of $n$ one observes that the harmonics $m=2,3,4,5,6$ are themselves among the top few Fourier peaks and can have normalized magnitudes comparable to $0.2$-$0.6$, suggesting a genuinely structured packet rather than a single isolated spike. This connects directly with the numerical claim in arXiv1507.00267 that, at large scales, the spectrum appears to be dominated by (essentially) two peaks and their harmonic footprints; in practice one can test this by ranking the values of $|S_n(k)|$ for $1\le k\le (p-1)/2$ and tracking the ranks of the harmonic family $mk_n^*$. A further experiment (useful for probing whether $\alpha$ admits any simple ''closed form'' approximation) is to fit the observed maximizers $\alpha_n$ by low-complexity models suggested by the $L$-packet picture, e.g. by approximating $\theta_n=\alpha_n/(2\pi)$ with short eventually periodic continued fractions (quadratic irrationals), or by rational multiples of $\pi$. Concretely, one may compare candidate angles $\widehat\alpha$ by computing the packet statistics $n^{-1}\big|\sum_{j\le n} e^{i m\widehat\alpha a_j}\big|$ for $1\le m\le L$ together with the sign-bias $\#\{j\le n:\cos(\widehat\alpha a_j)<0\}/n$, which is near $1$ for angles tightly aligned to the hidden signal. The main point is that these computations are straightforward to reproduce (generate $A_n$, choose $p>2a_n$, FFT the indicator of $A_n$), and they provide a concrete starting point for community exploration of packet structure, harmonic stability, and possible arithmetic constraints on the limiting frequency.

First of all, it's worth to add 3 main references on the problem: [1] D. Ross, The Ulam Sequence: Structure and Randomness, PhD thesis, University of Illinois at Urbana–Champaign, 2016 [2] S. Steinerberger. A Hidden Signal in the Ulam sequence. arXiv:1507.00267, 2015. [3] F. Clément and S. Steinerberger. Small Gaps in the Ulam sequence. arXiv:2501.16285, 2025 Steinerberger's results suggest the Ulam sequence exhibits a "hidden signal"—a strong Fourier peak at an irrational frequency $\alpha \approx 2.571$. Not much is known really and it seems the problem wasn't researched extensively. I managed to get an explanation with GPT-5.2 of why a "hidden signal" might arise and show some other heurestics towards the final answers. Below is a mean-field analysis explaining the stability of this signal and deriving a theoretical growth rate that closely matches empirical data (this is new, there was no explanation of this phenomenon before as I can see so far). You can see the full write up here. 1. The Fourier Setup Let $A_n = \{a_1, \dots, a_n\}$ be the Ulam sequence. Define the Fourier sum at frequency $\alpha$ as $S_n = \sum_{j=1}^n e^{i \alpha a_j}$. Empirically, $S_n$ grows linearly along the negative real axis: $S_n \approx -c n$ with $c \approx 0.79$. The number of representations of an integer $m$ as a sum of two terms, $s_n(m)$, is given via Fourier inversion (dominated by the DC term and the frequency $\alpha$):\[ s_n(m) \approx \frac{n^2}{q} + \frac{2 |S_n|^2}{q} \cos(\alpha m + \text{phase}(S_n)). \]For $S_n$ to be stable on the negative real axis, its phase is $\pi$. Thus:\[ s_n(m) \approx \frac{n^2}{q} \left( 1 + 2c^2 \cos(\alpha m) \right). \]2. The Phase-Locking Mechanism The Ulam rule selects the smallest $m > a_n$ such that $s_n(m) = 2$ (uniquely representable). Since the average representation count is large ($\sim n$), finding a value as low as $2$ requires "destructive interference" from the dominant mode to cancel the baseline.\[ 1 + 2c^2 \cos(\alpha m) \le 0 \implies \cos(\alpha m) \le -\frac{1}{2c^2}. \]This inequality creates a feedback loop: Existence Threshold: For a solution to exist, the cosine must be able to dip low enough. This requires $-1/(2c^2) \ge -1$, implying $c \ge 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$. The empirical $c \approx 0.79$ satisfies this stability criterion. Phase Trapping: The new term $a_{n+1}$ is forced to have a phase $\phi = \alpha a_{n+1}$ inside a specific "trough" arc centered at $\pi$. 3. The Self-Consistent Field Equation We can solve for the theoretical growth rate $c$. Let the "allowed arc" of phases be $[\pi - \delta, \pi + \delta]$. The boundary of this arc is defined by the threshold condition:\[ \cos(\delta) = \frac{1}{2c^2} \quad (*). \]Assuming the sequence samples phases uniformly within this allowed window (due to noise from other frequencies), the growth rate of the peak $S_n$ is the average projection of these new unit vectors onto the negative real axis:\[ c = \frac{1}{2\delta} \int_{-\delta}^{\delta} \cos(x) \, dx = \frac{\sin(\delta)}{\delta} \quad (**). \]4. Results and Comparison Solving the system of equations (*) and (**) numerically:\[ 2 \cos(\delta) \sin^2(\delta) = \delta^2 \]yields the solution:\[ \delta \approx 0.867 \text{ rad} \quad (49.7^\circ), \qquad c_{\text{theory}} \approx 0.879. \]This theoretical upper bound ($c \approx 0.88$) is remarkably close to the empirical value ($c \approx 0.79$). The discrepancy is naturally explained by "noise" ($L^2$ error terms) which effectively broadens the trapping window $\delta$, lowering the average cosine. 5. Implications for Open Problems Infinitely many pairs $ a, a+2 $: Likely. The non-uniform distribution on the circle (with positive density in the support arc) implies that phases recur infinitely often in regions where the next trough (gap) is exactly 2. Since $ \alpha $ is irrational, ergodicity ensures infinite visits to such phase sets. This is conjectural but fits the quasi-periodic picture. Eventually periodic differences: Unlikely. The irrational rotation on the circle (modulo the trapping arc) prevents strict periodicity in gaps. Density zero?: No—the persistent Fourier bias and positive $ c $ are inconsistent with zero density (which would imply more random-like behavior). Computations stabilize around $ \sim 0.076 $.

ChatGPT flagged some issues with the writeup. Since you've already produced two writeups that were immediately flagged this way, either: 1) my ChatGPT happened to be particularly inaccurate on results produced by your workflow, though I can't really see why; or 2) your workflow (human + AI as a unit) has some issues with verification of correctness.

Interesting, my ChatGPT agrees with this critique, even though writing everything one prior prompt before. I think this shows what's known for writing code with AI. Simplifying, often when you have a problem A in a bigger codebase and try to solve it with LLMs, you often introduce a problem B, and then when you try to solve problem B, you get problem A back. This seems what's happening here, when I have this longer conversations with LLMs and putting various pieces together that then become incosistent. Anyway, I'll rework it/simplify it. My main point here was to see whether we can have even conjecturally an explanation what is expected to happen here with this sentence, and I think "5. Implications for Open Problems" above are pretty good for that. Finally thanks for checking this that quickly! Feedback is always welcome to make some progress.
Thanks! That means the simple of strategy of asking ChatGPT to check its own work should work in your case too. (In my use, this often catches some minor errors by itself.)

Sure, and of course I do that multiple times, but that process almost never ends. You can do a funny exercise and ask ChatGPT about some paper that was well validated and accepted for publication (but without giving context on this, otherwise it gets influenced). If you ask to find gaps and errors in a paper, you're often get that even in verified/published papers. So at some point, if you're especially working on harder problems, you should stop relying on LLMs, and either use your own expertise or go with Aristotle to try to formalize it.

I get why you feel like that. In reality, just asking it to check its own work help tremendously. The approach is that it must *convince* you that the argument works (think peer review). This means you have to check some things, but it doesn't mean you need to check 100%. Of course, if the material is too hard and you can't become convinced/confident, then that's it; it doesn't work for that case. But there cases where it can work and save a lot of time.

In other circles people speak of "AI incest" when the same question on the same document is asked again and again. Instead of applying the same LLM-1 over and over again, it might help to apply several LLMs in turn (or in random order). An incest crisis occured with top computer chess programs around 2003 and 2004. A complete newcomer (Fruit by Fabien Letouzy) broke the "cycle" and become the symbolic father of new top programs like Rybka, Houdini and others.

Yep, in theory independent checks are better. But currently, GPT-5.2 is better with enough gap that self-check is sometimes better. But I also want to stress the key point - it must convince you, the human. That guards against this "blind spot" phenomenon, roughly up to the human's expertise level.

In my experience with LLMs, I found the best approach is to have 3 SOTA models check and evaluate each other, (e.g Gemini 3 + Opus 4.5 + gpt 5.2). Model 1 is the main discussion partner who exchange feedback with you and writes/takes notes. Model 2 checks what model 1 wrote then model 1 is fed the feedback from model 2 to evaluate and the process goes iteratively until Models 1 & 2 finally converge/agree on the same optimized output. Then you take that output and do the same process iteratively with model 3 optimizing further until you reach a state of convergence between the 3 peer-reviewers. The most important part in this process is obviously the human supervising them and assessing which model is being overly pedantic, which one is being overly agreeable, and which ideas/methods are actually worth pursuing. So it's essential to have sharp hallucination filters.
